concise:
Ayah 1–9 · linked video: 1. Al-Humazah (Ayah 1-9)

Surah Al-Humazah: Commentary on Ayahs 1-9

Ayah 1

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ

The connection between Surah Al-Asr and Surah Al-Humazah is remarkable. While Surah Al-Asr establishes that every human being is in loss, Al-Humazah takes this concept further by describing those who are in the worst kind of loss. This surah begins and ends with descriptions of hell, making it clear that we are dealing with the gravest of spiritual dangers.

The word (وَيْلٌ) carries tremendous weight. It represents a curse so severe, so beyond ordinary description, that the Arabic language leaves it undefined and mysterious - indicated by the tanwin (nunation). Some scholars describe wayl as a specific valley in hell so terrible that even hell itself is said to fear it. Others understand it as an expression of extreme frustration and anger. In the Quran, Allah ﷻ uses this word to convey the highest degree of divine displeasure.

The two qualities described here - humazah and lumazah - are not two different types of people, but rather two manifestations of the same diseased personality. The construction “humazatin lumazah” (without a connecting “wa” between them) tells us these are two sides of the same coin.

The dominant understanding among scholars is that humazah refers to someone who attacks others behind their backs, while lumazah describes someone who insults people to their faces. Imam Ibn Kathir notes that humazah can include subtle forms of mockery: the way someone looks at you, their facial expressions, a dismissive gesture, or the contempt visible in their eyes. Lumazah, on the other hand, involves direct verbal attacks and disparaging remarks.

Umm Al-Mumineen Aisha (RA) used a beautiful methodology to understand these words - she looked at how Allah ﷻ uses them elsewhere in the Quran. When the Quran mentions “hamazat ash-shayatin” (the whisperings of the devils), it refers to secret whispers, suggesting humazah is about speaking behind someone’s back. When hypocrites openly criticized the Prophet ﷺ about the distribution of charity, the word “yalmizuka” (from lamz) was used, indicating lumazah refers to open criticism.

The root of humazah comes from the imagery of a powerful bow pulled tight. When it releases an arrow, it penetrates its target precisely and painfully at the point of impact. This is someone who knows exactly what to say to wound another person, who targets their vulnerabilities with precision, stabbing at their dignity and self-worth.

Now we must ask: why does Allah ﷻ begin by condemning this behavior rather than shirk, murder or theft? Because the dignity of the believer is sacred to Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ once stood before the Kaaba during tawaf and said, “How pure you are, how good your scent is, how grand your sanctity. By the One who has Muhammad’s life in His hands, the dignity of the believer is more sacred to Allah than your dignity.” He was pointing at the Kaaba - that house whose liberation was the entire mission of his prophethood - and declaring that a believer’s honor is more sacred to Allah ﷻ than even the Kaaba.

The Prophet ﷺ continued: the believer’s wealth, blood, and honor are sacred. Then he said something profound about the third element: “We should think nothing but good about him.” Even our thoughts about other believers must be guarded. This is why believers are described in the Quran as “humble before one another” (adhillah) - when you are truly humble before someone, you cannot think of criticizing them. You think only of your own shortcomings.

Consider also that when Aisha’s (RA) honor was attacked through slander, Allah ﷻ revealed Quranic verses to defend her. She later reflected that she never thought herself worthy of having Allah’s ﷻ own words revealed about her situation - perhaps a dream shown to the Prophet ﷺ, but not Quran. Yet to protect her dignity, Allah ﷻ sent the higher form of revelation. Meanwhile, to restore the Kaaba to Muslim hands, He sent the Prophet ﷺ a dream. The protection of a believer’s dignity warranted Quran; the liberation of the Kaaba warranted a vision. This shows us the weight Allah ﷻ places on our treatment of one another.

When the Quran describes someone committing shirk or saying something blasphemous about Allah ﷻ, it instructs us to respond with “Subhan Allah.” But when believers heard slander about Aisha (RA), the Quran asked: “Why didn’t you say ‘Subhanaka’ (You are perfect, O Allah) when you heard it?” The same term used for blasphemy against Allah ﷻ was used for an attack on a believer’s honor. The conversation should have stopped immediately with recognition that this is an offense against Allah ﷻ Himself.

This helps us understand why wayl - why such severe anger - is directed at these people. When we hurt another believer, we commit a crime not merely against that person, but against Allah ﷻ who honored them, who created them, who declared their dignity sacred.

Why do people become consumed with pointing out others’ flaws? Deep insecurity and lack of self-respect drive this behavior. When you have no respect for yourself, when you see no dignity in your own character, the only way to feel superior is to push others down. You cannot elevate yourself, so you dedicate yourself to lowering others. This is actually a manifestation of arrogance - kibr. The Prophet ﷺ warned that no one with even an atom’s weight of arrogance will enter Paradise. Arrogance means seeing yourself as better than others, and constantly criticizing others is precisely how that arrogance manifests.

Ayah 2

ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًا وَعَدَّدَهُۥ

This ayah now describes another dimension of this diseased personality. After telling us about their treatment of others, Allah ﷻ reveals their relationship with wealth. The relative pronoun “alladhi” (the one who) connects this directly to the previous description - this is the same person who demeans others.

“Jama’a malan” - he gathered wealth. The verb implies constant accumulation, hoarding, bringing it all together for himself. Then comes “addadahu” - he counted it repeatedly. The word ’addada implies not just counting once, but counting again and again and again, obsessively verifying it, reviewing it, making sure it’s all there.

But ’addada carries another meaning: he made others count it too. He ensured people knew about his wealth. At parties, in conversations, he finds ways to mention his new car, his property, his assets. “I can’t believe I just got the new model - I don’t even have room in my garage anymore!” This isn’t just about having wealth; it’s about making sure everyone knows you have it, making sure it gets counted, tallied, noticed, envied.

Some people have essentially become walking advertisements for brands. They’re not individuals with character and substance - they’re coat hangers for labels, living billboards announcing their possessions. This obsession with material display is compensation for a void inside that remains unfilled no matter how much they acquire or show off.

Why this obsession with wealth? Because they’ve determined their worth by their possessions rather than the content of their character. They no longer see themselves as having intrinsic value that Allah ﷻ gave them. Instead, they need external validation through material things. The more they have, the more they can display, the more worthy they believe they are.

Our culture constantly reinforces this lie - that your worth equals your financial worth. If you don’t have an impressive degree, a high-status job, the right house, the right car, then who are you to speak? What do you have? This message gets internalized so deeply that even poor people begin to think this way. When they finally get some money, they immediately spend it on something to display rather than on what they actually need, because showing worth has become more important than genuine wellbeing.

But Allah ﷻ doesn’t measure human worth this way. A person sleeping under the stars in a desert, hungry for days, may be infinitely more dignified to Allah ﷻ than a billionaire. Yet the billionaire - and those dazzled by wealth - can become so deluded by the false image they’ve created that they truly believe their money makes them superior. This is what the Quran calls “mukhtaal” - someone on a high horse, having a lofty opinion of themselves that exists only in their imagination.

Ayah 3

يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ

“Yahsabu” means he assumes, he thinks, he calculates. Notice the irony - this word shares the same root as counting (hasaba), connecting back to how he counted his wealth. In his calculation, he has convinced himself of something absurd: that his money has made him eternal.

Look carefully at the verb tense. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “his money will make him eternal” (sa-yukhliduhu). He says “akhladahu” - his money has already made him eternal. It’s in the past tense, suggesting it’s already done, already accomplished. What does this mean?

It means he lives as though death isn’t real, as though his current state will never change. He doesn’t think about mortality. His mind goes from weekend to weekend, party to party, acquisition to acquisition. Between these moments, when thoughts about real purpose or meaning try to surface, he drowns them out with entertainment. He’s drugged himself with materialism more effectively than any substance could do.

Or he tells himself that his money will immortalize him through legacy - buildings with his name, institutions bearing his family name, monuments that will make people remember him forever. The Quran addresses this elsewhere: “You make carvings and monuments hoping to live forever” - as if stone structures could grant eternity to your name or meaning to your existence.

This attitude was captured perfectly in Surah Al-Kahf with the man who looked at his gardens and said, “This will never go away” (lan tabida abada). He couldn’t conceive of losing what he had. That’s not just thinking you’ll live a million years - it’s acting like bad times will never come, like you’re permanently set, like nothing could ever change your situation. It’s the ultimate delusion of those intoxicated by wealth.

Ayah 4

كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ

“Kalla!” - Absolutely not! No! This is a strong, emphatic negation. You’ve got it completely wrong, and now let me tell you what’s really going to happen.

“Layunbadhanna” - he will absolutely, certainly be cast away. The verb “nabdh” is what you do with worthless trash. When you’re sitting in your car eating chocolate and there are crumbs and wrappers on the seat - you brush them off without thought. When there’s a dirty napkin or garbage on the ground, you sweep it away. That’s nabdh - disposing of something that has no value, that’s beneath consideration.

Allah ﷻ is saying: this person who thought they were worth so much, who made others feel worthless, who believed their wealth gave them ultimate value - they will be thrown away like garbage. You thought you were something? You’re just trash.

“Fil-hutamah” - into the Crusher. The word “hutamah” comes from “hatm,” which refers to tiny fragments of dried leaves that people have stepped on until they’ve disintegrated into barely visible flakes. When you walk on hutam, do you feel like you’re stepping on something valuable? Do you carefully avoid it? No - it’s by definition meant to be stepped on. It’s worthless.

Allah ﷻ describes this person’s destination not just as hellfire, but specifically as the place that crushes worthless things. Both the verb (cast like trash) and the noun (the crusher) emphasize their utter worthlessness. Why? Because all they did in the first two ayahs was establish their false sense of worth - through putting others down and through wealth accumulation. Now Allah ﷻ strips away that delusion completely.

The word hutamah appears elsewhere in the Quran. The ants in Surah An-Naml feared being crushed (yahtimannakum). The term is used for dried, peeled-off layers - like the skin of a boiled egg that’s discarded. Camels are called “hutumah” because they crush whatever is in front of them as they walk. Now Allah ﷻ uses this word for a portion of hell.

Ayah 5

وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ

“And what will make you understand what the Crusher is?” This interrogative style increases the mystery and horror. The word “hutamah” was unfamiliar to the Arabs as a name for hell - they might have thought it referred to crushing animals like camels. So Allah ﷻ poses this question to create anticipation and dread. What could this crusher be?

Ayah 6

نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ

The answer: the fire of Allah, the one that has been kindled. This is the only place in the entire Quran where Allah ﷻ attributes fire directly to Himself with this possessive construction. Not just “fire” but specifically “Allah’s fire.” Do you grasp how angry Allah ﷻ is? Do you understand that this crime - though it seems to be against people - is actually being committed against Allah ﷻ Himself?

“Al-muqadah” - the one that has been lit and continues to be fueled. The verb “awqada” means to fuel a fire. The form “muqadah” (passive participle) means fire that is continually, perpetually being fueled. It never runs out of fuel. It keeps burning and burning and burning.

Notice the painful irony: this person’s greed was endless. They kept gathering and gathering, accumulating and displaying, never satisfied, always wanting more. Their heart was filled with limitless desire for wealth. So Allah ﷻ gives them a fire with limitless fuel. That heart that was consumed by endless greed will now be consumed by endless flames. The punishment mirrors the crime.

Ayah 7

ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ

which climbs up upon the hearts. The verb “tattali’u” means to climb upward. Think about this: if they’re in the Crusher being crushed, where are they positioned? At the bottom. So we expect punishment from above, pressing down. But Allah ﷻ says the fire climbs up to their hearts - meaning it also comes from below.

They’re trapped between two directional punishments. From above, fire crushes them. From below, fire crawls up toward their hearts like a snake or insect climbing their body, penetrating into the core of their being.

Why specifically the hearts? Because they hurt other people’s hearts. Because they carried love of wealth in their hearts where love of Allah ﷻ should have been. The heart was the real criminal. Yes, the tongue spoke the hurtful words. Yes, the eyes gave the contemptuous looks. Yes, the hands made the insulting gestures. But all of these external manifestations came from a corrupted heart. So the punishment goes to the source - the heart that was the root of all these crimes.

The word used here is “af’idah” (plural of fu’ad). While “qalb” is the general word for heart, “fu’ad” specifically refers to a heart that is intensely engaged, overwhelmed with strong emotion - anger, fear, love, extreme sadness. When your heart is in an intense, burning state, it becomes fu’ad. Allah ﷻ chooses this word because the heart will be literally and figuratively on fire.

There’s a profound figurative reading here as well. Every person you hurt, every heart you wounded, every individual whose dignity you trampled - you didn’t feel their pain when you did it. Had you felt it, had you truly understood what you were doing, you wouldn’t have done it. But on that Day, you will feel it. All the pain you caused will combine into a concentrated flame inside your heart. You will finally feel what they felt, and you’ll feel it eternally. This is the fire climbing into your heart - the accumulated agony you inflicted on others, now turned back upon you.

Ayah 8

إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ

indeed, it is closed tight upon them. The word “mu’sadah” comes from “asada,” which means to shut something completely, to seal it airtight. Think of a door locked so tight nothing can get through, or a container sealed so perfectly that it’s vacuum-packed.

This fire that’s crushing them from above and climbing up from below - it’s also hermetically sealed around them. There’s no gap, no opening, no room to move, no way to escape. It’s claustrophobic, suffocating, absolutely airless.

Why this specific punishment? Remember the principle in Islamic theology: Allah’s ﷻ punishments are precisely calibrated to the crimes committed. They suffocated others. They took wealth that rightfully belonged to others - family members who deserved support, employees who deserved fair wages, people who deserved compassion - and kept it all for themselves, creating tightness and difficulty in other people’s lives.

Think about workers in some countries forced to labor 20 hours a day for minimal wages. If they get sick, if they’re late even five minutes, they’re kicked out, sent back to poverty with no recourse. Think about five or six cab drivers sharing one mattress in a single room, taking turns sleeping in four-hour shifts between their exhausting work schedules, sending whatever they can back to starving families. Meanwhile, the people profiting from their labor have wealth beyond imagination - wealth they couldn’t spend in a hundred lifetimes - but they refuse to pay fair wages or provide humane conditions.

A very small percentage of people control the vast majority of the world’s wealth, and because of this hoarding, the majority of humanity suffocates economically. If those who hoard let go of even half their wealth, the world would transform. But they can’t let go because their identity is wrapped up in accumulation. They created suffocation for others, so now it’s tightened upon them - “mu’sadah.”

Ayah 9

فِى عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ

“Fi ’amadin mumaddadah” - in columns extended endlessly. The word “’amad” (plural of ’amud) refers to pillars, the structural columns that hold up a building and bear the most weight.

There are two ways to understand this. The straightforward reading: they are trapped inside these pillars, or underneath them, being crushed by enormous weight. The heaviest part of any structure is the pillar itself - imagine being trapped under that, with all that pressure bearing down on you.

“Mumaddadah” means stretched out, extended. But Allah ﷻ didn’t use the simple form “mamdudah” (stretched). He used the intensive form “mumaddadah” - stretched beyond stretched, extended beyond extension, endless. These columns keep stretching upward perpetually, just as their greed stretched perpetually. The weight keeps increasing, the crushing continues without end.

Notice the beautiful - and terrifying - linguistic parallel. In ayah 2, the person “addadahu” (counted it repeatedly). Here the columns are “mumaddadah” (extended repeatedly). The pattern mirrors the crime. They endlessly counted their wealth; now they’re under endlessly extending columns.

Another possible reading: they’re in a prison cell, and when they look at the bars, they see columns stretching upward infinitely, reinforcing their eternal imprisonment. The word “’amad” also means something that doesn’t go away - it’s permanent, immovable, fixed. From this root we get “a’mada” - when you’re firmly determined about something. The columns represent permanence, and for these people, that permanence means eternal punishment.

Reflection on the Surah’s Structure

The structure of this surah is remarkable. At its center (ayah 4) stands the crushing - “kalla layunbadhanna fil-hutamah” - ultimate destruction, complete worthlessness. If something is crushed into hutam (tiny fragments), you cannot be more lost than that. This is the worst possible end.

Before this central declaration come three crimes: 1. Hurting people through condescension and mockery (humazatin lumazah) 2. Obsession with accumulating and displaying wealth (jama’a malan wa-’addadahu) 3. Living as though wealth grants eternity (yahsabu anna malahu akhladahu)

After the central declaration come three punishments: 1. Allah’s ﷻ fire that climbs up to the hearts (narullahi al-muqadah allati tattali’u ’ala al-af’idah) 2. Sealed tight upon them (innaha ’alayhim mu’sadah) 3. In columns extended endlessly (fi ’amadin mumaddadah)

Each punishment corresponds precisely to its crime: - They hurt others’ hearts → Fire reaches their hearts - They obsessed over expansion and accumulation → They’re compressed and sealed tight - They thought wealth gave them endless life → They’re under endlessly extending columns

Even the vocabulary shows this correspondence: - Humazah/Lumazah parallel Hutamah (same morphological pattern) - ’Addada (counted repeatedly) parallels Mumaddadah (extended repeatedly)

The middle section (ayahs 5-6) stands distinct: “What will make you understand what the Crusher is? Allah’s ﷻ fire that has been kindled.” These two elements bind the crimes to their punishments, showing that this is Allah’s ﷻ direct judgment.

Final Reflection

This surah doesn’t require that someone be a billionaire to be guilty of these crimes. You can have little and still be obsessed with it. You can show off within your small circle. The disease isn’t the amount; it’s what’s in the heart. Once love of wealth and obsession with status penetrate your heart, once putting others down becomes your way of feeling worthy, you’ve entered dangerous territory.

The surah leaves these descriptions open-ended. “Waylun likulli humazatin lumazah” - woe to every, to each, to any person with these qualities. It could be anyone. It could be you. It could be me. That’s why we must search our hearts, identify these diseases within ourselves, and work to remove them.

If you’re suffering at the hands of people like this - people who demean you, who use wealth to make you feel inferior, who crush your dignity - know that Allah ﷻ sees. He sees your pain, and He will hold them accountable with perfect justice. Your dignity is sacred to Him, more sacred than the Kaaba itself.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from becoming people who deserve to be crushed. May He purify our hearts of arrogance, greed and contempt for others. May He help us recognize the sacred dignity He placed in every believer, and may He make us among those who honor others as He has commanded. May we recite these ayahs not with mere tongues, but with hearts that truly understand their weight and live by their guidance.
deep:
Ayah 1–9 · linked video: 1. Al-Humazah (Ayah 1-9)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍۢ لُّمَزَةٍ

وَيْلٌ (waylun) [و-ي-ل]

The root و-ي-ل carries meanings of destruction, regret and great sorrow. وَيْل/Wayl is used to express extreme frustration, anger and curse. It’s an expression of profound regret and sadness with someone. In some classical interpretations, wayl is described as a specific place in hell - the worst part of hell which hell itself fears. The tanween (indefinite noun) here makes it mysterious and unquantifiable - suggesting a terrible curse beyond words that cannot be specified. This grammatical choice emphasizes the magnitude and horror of the punishment awaiting these people.

هُمَزَةٍ (humazah) [ه-م-ز]

The root ه-م-ز originally described a strong, tense bow - the kind that when pulled back shoots with extra force and penetrates its target deeply. هُمْز/Humz refers to the moment of penetration when an arrow hits - the most painful point of impact. By extension, humazah describes someone who stabs at people with their words or gestures, hitting them precisely where it hurts most - like poking or splintering someone at an exact target point. This refers to someone who criticizes and insults people behind their backs, speaking in ways that pierce and wound hearts. The pattern fu’alah (فُعَلَة) indicates intensity and persistence in this behavior.

لُمَزَةٍ (lumazah) [ل-م-ز]

The root ل-م-ز means to criticize, disparage or use condescending remarks against someone. Lumz/لُمْز can be done through facial expressions, gestures, body language or direct words - but it’s done openly, to someone’s face. The Quran uses this root when describing hypocrites who criticized the Prophet ﷺ openly about charity distribution (وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَلْمِزُكَ/wa minhum man yalmizuka). The pattern fu’alah matches humazah, indicating both are persistent, habitual behaviors - two sides of the same personality rather than two different people.

Ayah 2

ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًۭا وَعَدَّدَهُ

جَمَعَ (jama’a) [ج-م-ع]

The root ج-م-ع means to gather, collect or accumulate. Here it describes someone who obsessively gathers wealth, hoarding and accumulating without satisfaction. The past tense verb suggests completed action but the context (with عَدَّدَ/addada following) indicates ongoing, habitual gathering - never satisfied, always wanting more.

مَالًا (malan) [م-و-ل]

The root م-و-ل refers to wealth, property and possessions. مَال/Mal encompasses all forms of material wealth - money, property, goods. The word appears in an indefinite form, suggesting wealth in general or large amounts of wealth that this person obsesses over.

عَدَّدَهُ (addadahu) [ع-د-د]

The root ع-د-د means to count or number. The form ‘addada (Form II) intensifies the meaning - counting repeatedly, obsessively, over and over. It also carries the meaning of making others count it - ensuring people notice and acknowledge the wealth through showing off. The attached pronoun ’hu’ refers back to the wealth (mal). The repetition in the pattern suggests continuous, never-ending counting and recounting.

Ayah 3

يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُ

يَحْسَبُ (yahsabu) [ح-س-ب]

The root ح-س-ب means to think, suppose or calculate. يَحْسَبُ/Yahsabu indicates present/ongoing assumption - he continuously thinks or assumes. Interestingly, حَسَبَ/hasaba also relates to counting (like ’addada), creating wordplay between counting wealth and miscalculating one’s fate. The present tense emphasizes the ongoing delusion of this person.

أَخْلَدَهُ (akhladahu) [خ-ل-د]

The root خ-ل-د means to make eternal, give everlasting life or make something permanent. The Form IV أَخْلَدَهُ/akhladahu means “has made him eternal” or “has granted him eternity.” The past tense is striking - not “will make him eternal” but “has already made him eternal” - suggesting this person already lives as if immortally, with no concern for death or accountability. This reflects the mindset of someone completely absorbed in this worldly life, acting as though they’ll never die.

Ayah 4

كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ

لَيُنبَذَنَّ (layunbadhanna) [ن-ب-ذ]

The root ن-ب-ذ means to throw away or cast off something worthless - like throwing trash or garbage. The Form I passive يُنۢبَذُ/yunbadhu means “he will be thrown.” The lam of emphasis (la-) and the emphatic nun (-anna) create the strongest possible emphasis: “He shall most certainly be thrown.” This construction emphasizes the certainty and severity of the punishment. The word choice itself is degrading - it’s the verb used for discarding worthless trash, crumbs, or dirty napkins. After thinking himself so valuable, he’ll be thrown away like garbage.

الْحُطَمَةِ (al-hutamah) [ح-ط-م]

The root ح-ط-م means to crush, break into pieces or pulverize. حُطَمَة/Hutamah is an intensive form meaning “the Crusher” - something that crushes repeatedly and thoroughly. It describes dried leaves that have been stepped on and reduced to tiny, barely-visible flakes - things so worthless you don’t even notice stepping on them. الْحُطَمَة/Al-Hutamah becomes a name for a specific place or level of Hell characterized by crushing and breaking down. The definite article ‘al’ makes it a proper name for this particular punishment.

Ayah 5

وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ

أَدْرَاكَ (adraka) [د-ر-ك]

The root د-ر-ك means to reach, comprehend or grasp something. The Form IV أَدْرَاكَ/adraka means “made you know” or “informed you.” The rhetorical question “وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ/wa ma adraka” (and what will make you know/comprehend?) is a Quranic formula used to emphasize something mysterious, awesome or beyond normal comprehension. It draws attention to the gravity and incomprehensibility of what’s being described.

Ayah 6

نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ

نَارُ (naru) [ن-و-ر]

The root ن-و-ر relates to fire, light and burning. نَار/Nar is fire, but here it’s specifically identified as Allah’s fire (نَارُ اللَّهِ/naru Allahi), making this the only place in the Quran where fire is attributed directly to Allah with this possessive construction. This attribution emphasizes that the crime was committed against Allah ﷻ Himself, not just against people, and increases the severity and horror of the punishment.

الْمُوقَدَةُ (al-muqadah) [و-ق-د]

The root و-ق-د means to kindle, light or fuel a fire. The Form IV passive participle مُوقَدَة/muqadah means “that which has been kindled” or “lit up.” The form suggests continuous, ongoing fueling - fire that is constantly being fed and maintained, never diminishing. This corresponds to the endless greed of the person being punished - just as their greed was never satisfied, the fire fueling their punishment never stops.

Ayah 7

ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ

تَطَّلِعُ (tattali’u) [ط-ل-ع]

The root ط-ل-ع means to rise, appear, climb up or ascend. The Form V تَطَّلِعُ/tațțali’u means to climb up, rise up or penetrate upward. Here it describes fire climbing up toward or into the hearts - suggesting flames crawling like insects or snakes from below, penetrating into the core of the person. The present tense indicates ongoing, continuous action.

الْأَفْئِدَةِ (al-af’idah) [ف-ء-د]

The root ف-ء-د gives فُؤَاد/fu’ad, meaning heart - but specifically the heart in a state of intense emotion or when it’s being “roasted.” The verb فَأَادَ/fa’ada means to roast meat. Af’idah is the plural of fu’ad, referring to hearts that are overwhelmed, excited or inflamed. The choice of this word over qalb (general word for heart) emphasizes both literal roasting and the overwhelming emotional/spiritual torment. The hearts are targeted because that’s where the original crimes originated - hearts filled with greed instead of love for Allah, hearts that wanted to hurt others.

Ayah 8

إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ

مُؤْصَدَةٌ (mu’sadah) [أ-ص-د]

The root أ-ص-د means to close tightly, shut firmly or seal completely. مُؤْصَدَةٌ/Mu’sadah is the passive participle meaning “closed tightly upon” or “sealed shut.” It describes something airtight, like a door locked tight (أَصَدَ الْبَابَ/asada al-bab) or a container sealed so nothing can get through. The image is of claustrophobic imprisonment with no room to move, completely trapped. This punishment corresponds to how they created “tightness” in the world - hoarding wealth while others suffocated in poverty, causing others to feel trapped and suffocated in their circumstances.

Ayah 9

فِى عَمَدٍۢ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ

عَمَدٍ (amad) [ع-م-د]

The root ع-م-د refers to pillars, columns or supports that hold up a building. ’أَمَد/Amad (plural of ’amud) are the structural columns bearing the weight of a structure. These are the heaviest, most weight-bearing parts of a building. The imagery suggests they’re being crushed under enormous weight, or trapped within pillars themselves - the ultimate crushing corresponding to الْحُطَمَةُ/al-Hutamah (the Crusher).

مُمَدَّدَةٍ (mumaddadah) [م-د-د]

The root م-د-د means to stretch, extend or prolong. The Form II passive participle مُمَدَّدَة/mumaddadah means “stretched out” or “extended” - but the intensive Form II pattern suggests extreme stretching, stretching beyond stretching, endlessly extended. The word parallels ’addada (he counted repeatedly) from Ayah 2 - just as he endlessly counted and displayed his wealth, never satisfied, so the columns endlessly stretch, and the punishment never ends. The correspondence in word patterns emphasizes the precision of Divine justice - the punishment perfectly mirrors the crime.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

The tanween (indefinite noun marker) on waylun creates a sense of mystery and magnitude - it’s an unspecified, unquantifiable destruction. The structure “waylun likulli” (woe to every) makes this universal - not addressing a specific person but a whole category of people. The use of the particle “li” (to/for) indicates these qualities deserve this destruction.

The construction “humazatin lumazah” places two similar-sounding intensive patterns side by side without a conjunction (asyndeton). This grammatical choice (called badal) indicates these are two aspects of the same person, not two different people. If Allah had used “wa” (and) between them, it might suggest two separate individuals or groups. The absence of the conjunction emphasizes unity - this is one personality with two manifestations.

Ayah 2

The pairing of “jama’a malan wa-’addadahu” creates a powerful rhythm. The sequence moves from gathering (past tense, suggesting completed hoarding) to counting (also past but implying repetitive action). The pronoun “hu” attached to ’addada refers back to mal, creating tight grammatical cohesion.

The word order is significant - mal comes before the counting, emphasizing that the wealth itself is the object of obsession. The wa (and) between the two verbs suggests these are sequential, connected actions - not just gathering but then obsessing over it through counting.

Ayah 3

The present tense “yahsabu” contrasts with the past tense verbs in Ayah 2, shifting to his ongoing mental state and delusion. The use of “anna” (that) introduces indirect speech, revealing his internal thoughts.

The past tense “akhladahu” (has made him eternal) is rhetorically striking - we would expect future tense (“will make him eternal”) but Allah uses past tense to indicate this person already lives as if immortal. This grammatical choice exposes the delusion - in his mind, he’s already achieved immortality through wealth.

Ayah 4

The emphatic construction “la-yunbadhanna” uses both the lam of emphasis (la-) and the emphatic nun (-anna), creating the strongest possible assertion in Arabic. This is emphatic rejection of the previous assumption - “No! He will CERTAINLY be thrown!”

The word “kalla” (No! By no means!) is a powerful negation particle, forcefully rejecting what preceded it. The shift from the person’s assumption to Allah’s decree is marked by this sharp rhetorical turn.

The passive voice “yunbadhu” (he will be thrown) removes agency from the person - he doesn’t throw himself; he’s thrown by forces beyond his control, emphasizing his powerlessness and the worthlessness implied by the verb nabadha.

Ayah 5

The rhetorical question “wa ma adraka mal-hutamah” is a classical Quranic device for emphasis and creating suspense. The repetition of “mal-hutamah” from the question draws attention and builds anticipation for the answer that follows.

The structure “wa ma adraka ma X” followed by “X is…” creates a pattern of question and answer that engages the listener and emphasizes the gravity of what’s being described.

Ayah 6

The idafa (possessive construction) “naru Allahi” (Allah’s fire) is unique in the Quran. Typically, the Quran refers to “the Fire” (al-nar) or “fire of Hell” (نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ/naru jahannam), but never with this direct attribution to Allah. This grammatical construction emphasizes Allah’s ownership and direct involvement in this punishment, elevating the severity of the offense - it’s not just any fire, it’s HIS fire.

The passive participle “al-muqadah” indicates a continuous state - the fire that is constantly being kindled. The definite article “al” makes it specific - THE fire that has been lit.

Ayah 7

The relative pronoun “allati” (that which) begins a descriptive clause, maintaining grammatical flow while adding new information about the fire. The present tense “tattali’u” indicates ongoing, continuous action - the fire keeps climbing.

The prepositional phrase “’ala al-af’idah” (upon/into the hearts) specifies the target. The particle “’ala” can mean both “upon” and “into,” allowing for interpretation as fire climbing up toward the hearts or penetrating into them.

Ayah 8

The pronoun “innaha” (indeed it) provides emphasis and refers back to the fire. The particle “inna” adds emphasis to what follows.

“’Alayhim” (upon them) specifies who the closing/sealing affects - emphasizing they are trapped within it, not just near it.

The passive participle “mu’sadah” describes a state - it IS closed upon them, emphasizing the permanence and completeness of their entrapment.

Ayah 9

The prepositional phrase “fi ’amadin” can be understood two ways grammatically: they are IN columns (trapped within) or the fire is supported BY columns. The preposition “fi” typically means “in” which suggests they’re inside or among the columns.

“Mumaddadah” modifies “’amad” - these are stretched-out, extended columns. The intensive Form II pattern in mumaddadah mirrors the intensive pattern in ’addada (Ayah 2), creating verbal symmetry between the crime (endless counting) and punishment (endless columns). This grammatical parallel emphasizes the precision of Divine justice.

Historical & Biblical Context

General Context

Surah Al-Humazah is a Makkan surah revealed in the early period of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission. During this time, Makkan society was characterized by extreme wealth disparity, materialism and tribal arrogance. The wealthy elite of Quraysh controlled trade routes and accumulated vast wealth, while many in society struggled with poverty.

The qualities described in this surah - backbiting, slandering, material obsession and arrogance - were widespread among the Makkan aristocracy who opposed the Prophet ﷺ . Some classical scholars mention this surah was revealed about specific individuals known for these traits, though the language remains general to include all who exhibit these characteristics.

Ayah 1-2

The description of humazah and lumazah reflects a society where honor and shame culture was dominant. People would attack others’ dignity through mockery, gossip and public insult. The gathering and counting of wealth (’addada) reflects the materialistic values of Makkan merchant society, where worth was measured by possessions and status.

In the Jahiliyyah period (pre-Islamic Arabia), displays of wealth were central to social standing. The obsession with counting and displaying wealth described here was not unusual but rather the norm among the elite. They would boast about their possessions, livestock and trade goods to establish superiority.

Connection to Previous Scriptures

The condemnation of backbiting and slander echoes biblical teachings. The Torah prohibits bearing false witness and speaking ill of others (Exodus 20:16, Leviticus 19:16). The emphasis on protecting human dignity and the prohibition of gossip appears throughout Jewish law and prophetic teachings.

The warning against trusting in wealth echoes numerous biblical passages, particularly in the Psalms and Jesus’s teachings about the difficulty of the wealthy entering the Kingdom of Heaven (Matthew 19:23-24). The Quran’s emphasis on wealth being temporary and the reality of accountability parallels these earlier scriptural warnings.

Ayah 4-9

The vivid description of punishment in Al-Hutamah reflects the Quranic pattern of making abstract spiritual realities concrete and comprehensible. The imagery of crushing, fire climbing to hearts and sealed imprisonment uses physical metaphors to communicate spiritual and emotional torment.

The concept that punishment corresponds precisely to the crime reflects the Quranic principle of perfect Divine justice - recompense is exact, not excessive. This principle appears throughout Quranic descriptions of the Hereafter and was a correction to both Arab and Jewish concepts of collective punishment or disproportionate retribution.

Coherence

Connection to Surah الْعَصْرِ/Al-’Asr

Surah Al-Humazah follows Surah Al-’Asr thematically and argumentatively. Al-’Asr establishes the universal principle that every human being is in loss (فِي الْخُسْرِ/fil-khusr) except those who believe, do righteous deeds, exhort each other to truth and exhort each other to patience. This is a general statement about the human condition.

Al-Humazah specifies WHO is in the WORST kind of loss. After establishing that everyone is losing (time, life, opportunity), Allah now describes the most terrible manifestation of that loss - those who hurt others’ dignity while obsessing over material wealth. This surah answers the implicit question: “If everyone is in loss, who experiences the most severe loss?” The answer: those who combine arrogance toward people with material obsession.

The word “khusr” (loss) from Al-’Asr connects to the imagery of utter worthlessness in Al-Humazah - being thrown away like trash (nabadha), crushed into nothing (hutamah). This is total, complete loss - not partial loss but annihilation of worth.

Internal Coherence

The surah has a precise internal structure centered on Ayah 4 “Kalla layunbadhanna fil-hutamah” (No! He will be thrown into the Crusher). This central statement is surrounded by:

  • Three preceding ayahs describing three crimes: hurting people’s dignity (humazah/lumazah), gathering wealth obsessively (jama’a malan wa-’addadahu), and assuming immortality (yahsabu anna malahu akhladahu)

  • Four following ayahs describing three corresponding punishments: being thrown worthlessly, Allah’s ﷻ fire penetrating hearts, being sealed in claustrophobic imprisonment, and endless crushing under stretched columns

Each crime has a corresponding punishment that mirrors it precisely: 1. Hurting hearts → Fire penetrating hearts (tattali’u ’alal-af’idah) 2. Endless gathering/expanding → Sealed/tightened upon them (mu’sadah ’alayhim) 3. Delusion of immortality → Endless punishment (fi ’amadin mumaddadah)

The word patterns also correspond: ’addada (counted repeatedly) mirrors mumaddadah (stretched endlessly), and humazah/lumazah (similar patterns) corresponds to hutamah (same pattern family).

Connection to Broader Quranic Themes

This surah develops the Quranic theme of the sanctity of human dignity, which appears prominently in Surahs like Al-Hujurat (49:11-12) which explicitly prohibits mockery, suspicion and backbiting. The command “وَلَا يَغْتَبْ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا/wa la yaghtab ba’dukum ba’dan” (do not backbite one another) in الْحُجُرَات/Al-Hujurat is elaborated here with consequences.

The contrast between worldly wealth and spiritual worth connects to the story of Qarun in Surah Al-Qasas (28:76-82), where someone blessed with wealth becomes arrogant and dismissive of others, ultimately being swallowed by the earth. Both narratives emphasize that material abundance without spiritual humility leads to destruction.

The surah also connects to the Quranic emphasis on the Hereafter being the realm where true values are revealed - what seemed valuable in this world (wealth, status, power to hurt others) becomes worthless, while what seemed insignificant (humility, kindness, protection of others’ dignity) becomes precious.

Position in the Quran

As a Makkan surah, Al-Humazah belongs to the period when the Quran was establishing fundamental moral and spiritual principles. The emphasis on social ethics (not hurting others) alongside warnings about materialism addresses the core problems of Makkan society that opposed the Prophet’s ﷺ message.

The placement in the final جُزْ/juz’ (30th section) means these powerful warnings are among the most frequently recited passages, ensuring believers regularly reflect on their treatment of others and their relationship with wealth.

Psychological, Political & Social

Psychological Insights

The Psychology of Insecurity

The behaviors described - humazah and lumazah - emerge from deep psychological insecurity and lack of self-worth. When someone doesn’t respect themselves or see inherent value in themselves, they attempt to feel superior by diminishing others. This is not confidence but compensation for inner emptiness.

Putting others down (whether behind their back or to their face) is a desperate attempt to elevate oneself without doing the internal work of genuine growth. It’s easier to hold someone else down than to build yourself up. This reveals the psychological principle that those who truly respect themselves have no need to disrespect others.

Material Obsession as Existential Avoidance

The behavior of gathering and counting wealth obsessively (jama’a malan wa-’addadahu) represents an attempt to fill an internal void with external possessions. This is a form of existential avoidance - instead of confronting the emptiness within, the person constantly accumulates and displays possessions.

The psychology here is profound: when you can’t find value in your character or soul, you seek value in possessions. Your worth becomes determined not by who you are but by what you have. This creates an endless cycle because possessions never truly satisfy spiritual and psychological needs. No matter how much you accumulate, the void remains.

The Delusion of Immortality

The mindset “yahsabu anna malahu akhladahu” (he assumes his wealth has made him eternal) represents psychological denial of mortality. By living as if there’s no death, no accountability, no end, the person avoids confronting the temporary nature of life and the need for meaningful purpose.

This delusion manifests as living only for immediate gratification, endless expansion and short-term thinking without regard for long-term consequences or ultimate accountability. It’s a form of psychological escapism from the reality of death and judgment.

Social Dynamics

The Destructive Nature of Backbiting

The emphasis on humazah (backbiting) and lumazah (open insult) highlights how destructive these behaviors are to social cohesion. When people constantly criticize, mock and demean others, it creates an atmosphere of fear, mistrust and division.

The Prophet ﷺ taught that the dignity of a believer is more sacred than the Ka’bah itself. This establishes that the foundation of Islamic society must be mutual respect, protection of reputation and benefit of the doubt toward others. Without this, community disintegrates.

The distinction between humazah (behind the back) and lumazah (to the face) shows that both forms of disrespect are equally condemned. Someone who insults to your face thinking they’re more “honest” than those who gossip is equally guilty. Both destroy dignity and social bonds.

Wealth Disparity and Social Justice

The critique of someone who gathers and hoards wealth while others suffer points to issues of social justice and wealth distribution. The Islamic principle is that wealth comes with responsibility - those who have much must support those who have little.

The behavior condemned here - gathering endlessly while creating “tightness” for others through economic exploitation, unfair wages or hoarding - directly opposes the Quranic vision of a just society where wealth circulates and the vulnerable are protected.

The surah critiques not wealth itself but: (1) making wealth the measure of human worth, (2) obsessing over accumulation at the expense of human welfare, and (3) using wealth to establish superiority over others. These attitudes poison social relations and create systemic injustice.

Political Implications

Critique of Power Structures

While the surah doesn’t explicitly address politics, its critique of those who demean others while hoarding wealth applies to political systems that concentrate power and resources among elites while marginalizing the majority.

Historically, the Makkan elite who opposed Islam represented precisely this combination: economic power used to maintain social hierarchy, combined with mockery and persecution of those (like early Muslims) who challenged their authority. The surah thus contains an implicit critique of oppressive power structures.

Human Dignity as Foundation

By establishing that attacking human dignity is among the worst sins deserving severe punishment, the surah lays groundwork for a political philosophy centered on inherent human worth. No one - regardless of wealth, lineage or status - has the right to demean another human being.

This principle, if implemented politically, would prohibit systems that dehumanize people based on class, race, ethnicity or economic status. It would require leaders and systems to protect the dignity of all people, especially the vulnerable.

Contemporary Relevance

Social Media and Modern Humazah

The digital age has created unprecedented platforms for humazah and lumazah. Social media enables backbiting on a massive scale - people can mock, criticize and demean others behind screens, spreading gossip instantly to thousands.

The anonymity and distance of online communication removes natural inhibitions about hurting others. People say things online they would never say face-to-face, forgetting that the spiritual and moral implications are identical. The surah’s warnings apply fully to digital backbiting and cyberbullying.

Consumerism and Material Obsession

Modern consumer culture actively encourages the mentality condemned in this surah - constantly accumulating possessions, showing them off, measuring worth by material success. Advertising, social media and economic systems push people toward the mindset of gathering and displaying wealth obsessively.

The psychological harm is evident: epidemic levels of depression, anxiety and sense of worthlessness despite material abundance in wealthy societies. People have more possessions than ever but feel emptier than ever, constantly comparing themselves to others’ displays of wealth.

Economic Inequality

The surah’s critique speaks powerfully to contemporary economic inequality where a tiny percentage of humanity controls vast wealth while billions struggle with poverty. The mindset of endless accumulation without concern for others’ welfare - thinking wealth makes one superior - characterizes much of modern capitalism.

The Islamic alternative presented implicitly here is that wealth comes with social responsibility, that human worth is intrinsic not economic, and that dignity must be protected especially for the economically vulnerable.

Quranic Wisdom

The Sacred Nature of Human Dignity

The fundamental teaching of this surah is that human dignity is sacred to Allah - more sacred even than the Ka’bah. The Prophetﷺ taught this while doing tawaf around the Ka’bah, pointing to it and saying the dignity of a believer is greater to Allah than its sanctity. This is stunning when we consider that liberating the Ka’bah was the central mission of his prophethood.

What does this teach us? That our relationship with Allah cannot be separated from how we treat His creation. You cannot claim to honor Allah while dishonoring those He has honored (“وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِي آدَمَ/wa laqad karramna Bani Adam” - We have honored the children of Adam). When we hurt another person’s dignity through mockery, gossip or condescension, we’re not just hurting them - we’re committing an offense against Allah Himself.

This is why the surah uses “naru Allahi” (Allah’s fire) - the only time fire is attributed directly to Allah this way in the Quran. The message is clear: this crime is against Him. When you diminish someone Allah has dignified, you’re attacking His decree, His creation, His handiwork.

The Emptiness of Materialism

The deep wisdom here is recognizing that material possessions can never fulfill spiritual needs. When you try to fill an internal void with external things - no matter how expensive, impressive or abundant - you will always remain empty. This is because human beings were created for something higher than accumulation.

Allah created us to long for beauty, comfort and abundance - but He did so as a preview of Jannah, not as an end in itself. The love of beautiful things should point us toward the eternal beauty of Paradise, not trap us in endless worldly pursuit. When material success becomes the goal rather than a means, we’ve lost our way.

The person described thinks their wealth has made them eternal (أَخْلَدَهُ/akhladahu). This reveals a spiritual blindness - trying to achieve immortality through legacy, monuments, accumulated wealth, names on buildings. But true immortality comes from preparing for the eternal life after death, not pretending this temporary life is forever.

Self-Respect and Respect for Others

There’s a profound connection between how we view ourselves and how we treat others. When you truly understand your worth comes from being Allah’s creation, honored by Him, given purpose and potential - not from possessions or status - then you can recognize that same divine gift in everyone else.

Conversely, if you don’t respect yourself, if you feel worthless inside, you’ll desperately try to feel superior by putting others down. The person who backbites and slanders is actually revealing their own deep insecurity. This is why the surah connects the two problems - hurting others and material obsession - they’re both attempts to compensate for internal emptiness.

True strength is recognizing you don’t need to diminish anyone else to have value. True wealth is finding contentment in what you have while maintaining generosity toward others. True dignity is Allah’s gift to you simply for being human - it can’t be taken away by poverty and isn’t increased by riches.

The Mirror of Divine Justice

One of the most striking aspects of this surah is how precisely the punishments mirror the crimes. You hurt people’s hearts with your words? Fire will penetrate your heart. You created tightness for others through hoarding? You’ll be sealed in claustrophobic imprisonment. You endlessly counted and displayed wealth? You’ll face endless, stretched-out columns crushing you.

This teaches us that Allah’s justice is perfect - not excessive, not arbitrary, but precisely calibrated. The punishment fits the crime exactly. More deeply, it shows that the spiritual realities we create here manifest in concrete form in the Hereafter. The internal ugliness that we mask in this world becomes visible and tangible in the next.

Living with Consciousness of Death

The delusion that wealth brings immortality (yahsabu anna malahu akhladahu) is really about living without consciousness of death. When you forget you will die, when you ignore accountability, when you live only for now - you make terrible choices. You hurt people carelessly, you hoard selfishly, you waste your life on worthless pursuits.

The antidote is remembering death frequently (as the Prophet ﷺ taught: “Remember often the destroyer of pleasures”). When you remember you’ll stand before Allah, you become careful with people’s dignity because you know you’ll answer for every word. When you remember you can’t take your wealth with you, accumulation loses its power over you.

This isn’t meant to make you depressed but to make you wise - to help you invest in what lasts (good deeds, strong relationships, positive impact) rather than what disappears (possessions, status, the approval of others).

Practical Guidance for Daily Life

Guard Your Tongue and Heart: The surah warns us to be extremely careful about how we speak about others, whether they can hear us or not. Before speaking about someone, ask: Would I say this if they were present? Would I want someone saying this about me? Most importantly, am I committing a crime against Allah by diminishing someone He has honored?

Check Your Relationship with Wealth: Material comfort isn’t wrong, but using it to define your worth or establish superiority is spiritually deadly. Regularly assess: Am I grateful for what I have? Do I share generously? Has my wealth made me arrogant or humble? Do I see others as less valuable because they have less?

Cultivate True Self-Worth: Recognize your value comes from being Allah’s creation, made with purpose, capable of growth, worthy of dignity. Not from your possessions, status, appearance or others’ opinions. When you root your worth in Allah’s valuation of you, you become free from the need to put others down or show off.

Protect Others’ Dignity Actively: Don’t just avoid backbiting - defend others when they’re being slandered in your presence. The moment you hear inappropriate talk about someone, say “SubhanAllah” - recognizing this is a crime against Allah. Change the subject. Leave the conversation if it continues. Your silence is participation.

Remember Death Daily: Make it a practice to remember you will die and face Allah. This isn’t morbid - it’s wisdom. It helps you prioritize what matters, treat people better, and invest in what’s eternal rather than what’s temporary. When you live with consciousness of the Hereafter, this life’s temporary pleasures and pains find their proper proportion.
